sp.p: Well, the first day I went into Paris....Paris hadn't fallen, so I had to get back. But I didn't...I had a very interesting time that first day...

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Are you saying that it was still under Nazi control?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Yeah, still under Nazi control. There was still so much shooting going on...I didn't see any. I could see there was some fighting going on up on the rooftops...I could hear planes flying down the river and a lot of activity on the river. The Nazis were fleeing, really.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And so when you got to Paris, you were with what unit?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: All by myself in my Jeep. I did pick up with an Army captain, at Chateau D'Un, who wanted to go into Paris with me. But we didn't know that Paris hadn't fallen yet. But you know the streets were beautiful...the red, white and blue...the American flag, the British flag and the French flag...all flying, all floors. It was magnificent. And I was right there, you know...right there on Rue St. Honore, where all the perfume shops were and I go into one...I'm on the street, I've parked my Jeep. I'm looking now...my wife wanted some La Donna perfume. Well, there was La Donna shop right there.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: That's what I was going to ask you...it was going to be actually my next question...which is, how did you stay in touch with your wife and your family during this whole time, from the time you enlisted to arriving in Paris and then after you left Paris until the time you got back to the United States. How did you...?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Just letters...APOs, letters...Navy.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And how often did you get mail?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Well, maybe...maybe every 10 days. And then you'd get a whole stack of mail...

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: At one time.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Yeah.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: They'd follow you around.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Yes. And... But I did meet at that bar...someone pulled me into a bar and it was Dutchess, an American Philadelphia girl that married a French duke. She was holding a cocktail party for all the Americans. And I finally found man next to me that was a little bit too fresh, I thought, so I got up to walk out and I thanked him and walked out...he followed me and then he nailed me against the wall. And I told him...he asked me something and I said, "It's none of your goddamned business," and I ducked and went on my way. And then finally...my Jeep was still parked there and I got the perfume and so forth. Oh, they gave you things, too. There's a ring that was given to me...a beautiful ring. My daughter-in-law has it now. But I get back to the bar and the Dutchess of D'Ouzes says, "What do you have against Ernest Hemingway?"

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Oh, so that was Ernest Hemingway?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: That was Ernest Hemingway that was too familiar.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: So you had some very special adventures. Now let me ask you this...you kind of told us how you entertained yourself, but while you were in the service, did you ever have the opportunity to see any of those USO shows with Bob Hope or...?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Not one.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Not any kind of organized entertainment.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Not once.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Even when you were at the front or anything like that?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: When I was at the...well I was near one once. I said I'd like to go and see the show and they said it's only for enlisted men...officers aren't allowed.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Really? That's interesting. Did you keep a diary while you were...?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: No, that was...you can't...it's illegal.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: O.K. Do you recall V-E Day...when you heard about V-E Day? Were you in Paris then?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: No, I was back in Washington.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: You had already gone back to Washington.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Yes, uh-huh. After Paris fell I had a choice to go home or SHAEF wanted me. Now SHAEF...I don't know what SHAEF stands for.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: It's o.k.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Huh?

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: We all know what SHAEF is.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: You know?

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Yes.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: I don't know.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: So you had a choice to either join up with SHAEF or go back to Washington. So from Paris you went to what...Le Havre?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: So from Paris...no, I went back to Cherbourg and then I notified the Navy that I was leaving and they wanted to come out and see my quarters. And when they saw the room I was in they said, "Your quarters are nicer than the admiral's." They said, "You should be ashamed of yourself living in such luxury with all these poor guys..." But I said, "It's all paid for by the Nazis."

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: O.K., so I now I want to cover from the time...where did you actually leave to go back to the United States to Washington?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: I left from North Ireland.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: From Ireland?

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Ireland, yes. I had to...they flew me over to Ireland to take a...I was going home by a clipper. I had to wait one week before...in Ireland before I could be flown home.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: So you came back by air?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: I came back by air.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: O.K. and landed where? [End of tape two; beginning of tape three] You were telling me something about the thought that occurred to you about when you were over in the Cherbourg area.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Well, I've been at the front many times and it's very strange. War is not exciting like it is in the movies...that there are lulls and most of the war in time is a lull and then you have a hot and heavy battle, but it's brief and a long lull. So I've been to the front like that and it never bothered me because I've always had a lull. But I was at the front only once and it was in the Cherbourg area. It was north of Valognes and south of Cherbourg where the two armies were shelling each other, but the front was between the shelling and we....and be underneath the canopy of all this going on overhead, going back and forth...it can become very, very nervous and trying. I had no business being there because I'm not supposed to be in...and I thought...I always get ahead of myself. But...so I went back. But I felt I shouldn't have gone back...I should have stayed with those men. But I'm not a combat soldier...I had no weapon.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: You carried no weapons with you?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: The only weapon I had was a .45 pistol for self defense.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Oh, o.k...well, I'm glad you told me that about the story about the combat situation. Now let's go back to...you're in Paris, you receive orders to go back to London...

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: I didn't receive orders to go back to London, except I had been advised that you stay in France until Paris falls. And after Paris falls, we had targets in Paris who look at certain officers and so forth. But they were completely empty...there was no targets of any value. Then I go back to Cherbourg and I had orders waiting for me there....and for transportation to flyback...

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: To London.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: To London. And back in London it was wonderful. We found out we could have reservations at the Ritz Hotel.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: O.K., now when you were in London, was that when V-E Day occurred?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: No.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Where were you when V-E Day occurred?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Oh....that occurred after the Battle of the Bulge and that was months later. See, I was in Washington, D.C....Anacostia my home was...and my son was even born by that time, I believe. When was...

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Well, let's back up a minute. Let's just cover...you receive orders to go to London, o.k., and you get to London and how long were you there?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Oh, I was there just a couple of days.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And then on to Ireland.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: And then into Ireland.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And then you were flown back to the United States.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Flown back to the United States.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: In a PBY.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: In a PBY.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And do you recall where you landed?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: I remember flying over Boston....

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: O.K.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: ...and I'm sure we landed in the Washington, D.C. area in Chesapeake Bay...I'm quite sure because I didn't have to get out and take a train anyplace. I was just...was home.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Oh, o.k., so you were...so then basically you were in the Washington area and that was your assignment?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Yes.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And how long were you in Washington and where were you assigned to?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: I was assigned to the Photographic Intelligence Center and then...oh, as soon as I got there, though...______was sort of disappointed in me or surprised. He said, "You report downtown to a certain...in Washington, D.C..." There was the Loft Building and I had a whole floor and I had 500 Waves....oh, it couldn't be that many. But I had Waves...all from Japan. All American girls that had never been to the United States. They were assigned to me to make our harbor report and why that happened is when I was in Cherbourg, I wrote a letter to Forestal. I didn't go through the chain of command...I wrote a letter to the Secretary of the Navy telling him what was wrong with the invasion of Normandy. And right away I was in charge of an intelligence section there.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And how long did you do that?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: I was there up until they dropped the bomb on Hiroshima. Then that was over...that was the end of the war.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: So you were in Washington until they dropped the bomb on Hiroshima...and that was August 6,1945.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Uh-huh.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And then what happened...what was your reaction when you heard about the dropping of the bomb?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Where was I?

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: No, I said what was your reaction? What did you think? Did you know anything about it?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: I knew nothing about it, nor did I know how...what a big...I couldn't imagine any bomb could be that big to do the destruction that it did. It's beyond my comprehension. I'm not very smart in ordnance anyway.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: What did you think when you heard about it though?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Oh, I said, "All the work I did was for nothing." It broke my heart. I had all these harbor reports going and publications going and I told the Navy, "We should continue doing these harbor reports and we should always have a library in_____department of all the big ports all over the world and keep these harbor reports up to date."

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: But when the bomb was dropped, did you have any conception in your mind that this was going to end the war?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: No. I knew it would be over, yes. After two of them...there was the Hiroshima and the Nagasaki and...

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And then on the 14th is when Japan surrendered...of August...August 14th.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Yes.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And then after the war ended...

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: After the war ended, then it was waiting to be mustered out.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: O.K. and you...and so you waited from the end of the war, which was August 1945 until January 15,1946. So it took another number of months...

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Yes, it took a number of months to get squared away.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And were you in the Washington area all this time?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: In the Washington area, um-hmm.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And your wife was with you?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Yes, see, it so happened I had orders for Pearl. I was going to leave just before this all happened, you see. I had orders...but living in Washington...and I was in the car pool and there was a man in BUPERS, an officer, he said, "If you take your leave..."____going to Pearl, I needed a leave. "If you take your leave in Washington, D.C., then you won't have to go to Pearl and you'll be mustered out." And so I gambled on that and I stayed and I got my orders changed not to go to Pearl Harbor and they kicked me out of the Navy.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: How did you feel about that--leaving? Did that transition to civilian life...was that difficult?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: I thought it would be, but it wasn't. See, when I got...I'll tell it was wonderful. I went back to RKO. They had a...they sat down before the war...

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: So wait a minute...you were with RKO before the war?jHarvey A. Warren:: Oh, yes.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: All right, now...what did you do in the immediate days afterwards? Did you go immediately back to RKO or...?jHarvey A. Warren:: Well, first of course I drove from Washington to Memphis and visited my wife's folks. And then my son was ill and we went down to New Orleans because it's warmer and he got better and then we drove all the way through across Texas and then got into Southern California.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And your son was born while you were in Washington?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Yes, uh-huh.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And what is his name?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Harvey Alan Warren, Jr.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And did you go back to work immediately? Did you take some time off after leaving the service?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Well, after I got into...after my leave...well, I had my leave... I was out of the service. I don't know where my money came from, though. I don't know what happened. I do know this, though--I drove up to Seattle. We did a lot of driving because they hadn't seen the grandson. And they adored the grandson and everything was nice. And then I come home and I go to RKO.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And this was in what city?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: That's in Hollywood, California. I noted Charlie Pike and Charlie Pike, "Get back on the job."

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: O.K., and while you were in the service, did you make any close friendships that you continued after you left the service?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Yes, I made several but they didn't last very long, except one lasted for a long, long time.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And who was that?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: That was Captain...his name...Captain Kelley in the Army. We had no duty together...he was in Washington, D.C. and he was in finance and he was the controller of U.S. Steel.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And so you just developed a relationship with him while you________?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Well, we continued the relationship principally because it was convenient geographically. He was from Southern California and I was from Southern California, and they had their domestic problems and we helped them so much...Marge and I. We helped them. They separated and we got them back together again and...

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And did you ever join any veteran's organizations after you left the service?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Just the Retired Officers Association, and it was not really an active organization...just a magazine.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Veterans of Foreign Wars...did you belong to that?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: No, I didn't.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And let me backtrack a minute. Were you awarded any medals or citations for your service?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Yes.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Do you remember what those are?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Well, I had the European Theater of War...European Theater Ribbon and also an Admiral's Citation Ribbon.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: O.K.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: It seems like...two little ribbons...who wants just two little ribbons?

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Well, did they have Battle Stars on them?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Two... yeah, they had two stars...

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Two Battle Stars.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Uh-huh.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And...

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: But that was a citation, I thought.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Well, they're ribbons and each star is a Battle Star, so it showed that you were at D-Day, I imagine.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: At D-Day and...

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: You know better than I. And you got the Victory Medal, which was given to everyone who served, correct?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: I didn't get any Victory Medal.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Well, you're eligible for it, so maybe you should apply for it. That's not uncommon. Many veterans have medals that they earned that they never received. So, let me get a little philosophical in terms of your military experience...how it influenced you or influenced your thinking about the war or about the military in general. Do you have any thoughts on that?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Well, no. I couldn't be a military man. I couldn't stay in the Navy. Actually, it was a different...after the war it's different. When you have war you have....I just couldn't stay in the Navy...I couldn't. I couldn't even stay...the State Department wanted me in the worst way. They wanted me so badly that I could...they wanted me to join without any 37 examinations whatsoever. They kept a pretty good check on me. See, I was with FBI men and I knew S...O.S.S.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Well, with your intelligence training...

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: But I knew what they wanted me for. They wanted me for the CIA and I didn't...

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: You had no interest.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: I had...I didn't like the way they did things.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: So in a sense, then your military work did influence you. You knew you didn't want to do that type of thing anymore.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: No.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And how did your service and experiences affect your life after you left the service, do you think? What do you think about that? Thinking about your service...you military service...how do you think it affected your life after you left the service?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: I think I became much more serious. I have religious values. I'm more like moral values. My religion is sort of very private, but...I have a guilt complex and every once and awhile it hits me because Captain Ives should have been stopped and I should have been the one to stop him. See and that hits me all the time...the guilt, the shame, the guilt.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And you feel guilty for a decision he made?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: I feel guilty for not...not being capable of telling him not to go on this mission.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: But he was a superior officer to you...

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: He was.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Do you really think that you would have had any influence on his decision?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Well, for instance...I tried to influence him and I didn't. But I reached a point where I looked at all the men sitting there and taking on this skirmish and I thought, if I just took out my pistol and shot him in the leg I could have stopped the whole thing. But then what would happen to me? He could take me out and shoot me for being out of uniform.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Shoot him in the other leg.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: You can't win for losing.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: That's right. Well, I just as an aside, I don't think you should feel any guilt over that. This was a grown man who was a superior officer and he made the decision and frankly, I don't think anything you could have said or done would have changed his decision.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Well, you're saying exactly what Commander Marvin said to me. He said...and he was his closest friend. It was a...it was a strange friendship. Marvin was the Mayor of Portsmouth and he was the commodore of the yacht club and he was in the cream of society and in New England's social life and society is quite different than in the west here. And Marvin introduced him into that society. He...

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Well, is there anything besides this thought that you've just expressed about Captain Ives that affected your life after you left the service? Did it help or hinder your career in any way?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: I would say all experience is valuable and I'd say it's valuable because you can make use of it. And it's very valuable to me....it didn't hinder, it helped me. It helped me to perhaps be more thoughtful or...

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: To have a direction...more of a direction perhaps.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Yes...I could understand human relationships better. But I was at such a tremendous disadvantage when I went into Ives and being in the dirty, mud-splattered, needing a shave.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Those are some of the things that you remember now.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Yeah.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: The discomforts of war.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Yeah.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And as we come to the end of this interview, is there anyone that you would like to dedicate...any entity...grandchildren, children, comrades...that you would like to dedicate this recording to?

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Well, I think to my family....the Warren Family...all of them...all of them. I say all of them because I know my grandson is very much interested in all of this and my granddaughters are, too. And my daughter-in-law in Florida is...everyone is supportive.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Well, your story has been fascinating. Your involvement in the planning of D-Day is an amazing story and I thank you for contributing your thoughts on that.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: I have an ego and I would like to mark it somehow... "Snafu"...the story. I would like...and I'm not interested in financial gain. I'm interested in...I just hope that it was good enough to be published or be used in some way. I'd like it as a motion picture, of course.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: And "Snafu," of course, is your memoirs about D-Day.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Yes.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Well, I hope that happens for you. And on behalf of the Historical Society, I want to thank you for sharing your recollections of your World War II experiences with us. And I want to thank you for the service and the sacrifices you made in World War II. I hope that future generations will come to realize that the courage and sacrifice made by your generation that we enjoy the freedom that we have today. Because if it wasn't for your generation, the whole story would probably be very different.

sp.speaker: Harvey A. Warren
sp.p: Yes, when it happened...when it happened. I want to say that I...it's been a privilege and an honor to tell you of my memoirs. It really has been. And I appreciate it.

sp.speaker: Fran Foley
sp.p: Thank you. It's been my pleasure and it's a joy to know you. Thank you, Harvey.
[End of Interview]
